speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is January 7th, 1997. The survivor is Morris Rubenstein. The interviewer is Deborah Miller. We are in Livonia, Michigan, United States of America, and this is being conducted in English. I'm Deborah Miller, an interviewer for the Shoah Foundation. Today's date is January 7th, 1997, and I have the pleasure of interviewing Morris Rubenstein. We are in Livonia, Michigan in the USA, and we are conducting this interview in English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Can you tell me your first and last name and how you spell both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Rubenstein. I have to spell. M-R-E--

speaker: interviewer
text: M-- Morris is-- how do you spell Morris?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-R-R-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Rubinstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubenstein, R-U-B-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. And it's Rubinstein? You pronounce it Rubinstein?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rubenstein, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And was what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moshe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Moshe?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] Moshe is [HEBREW]-- In Hebrew?

speaker: interviewer
text: However you would like. In--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Hebrew, it's [HEBREW] shin, hey.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any other names or nicknames you went by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's 19-- I think it's 1924, '25, [INAUDIBLE], you know. I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: But also there's another date that they go by?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they mixed it up in the DP camp, you know, to 1928, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know the month or the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 11, 1928. You know, they mixed it up, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what is your age right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I-- well, it's 68 or 69, I guess, you know, on the 28th. But on the 1924 would be like 72, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what city and country where you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rozyszcze. Rozyszcze, but I don't know how to spell it. We'll--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That starts with an R?

speaker: interviewee
text: With an R, Rozyszcze.

speaker: interviewer
text: R-O-J-I-S-T maybe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it could be. R-Z-- could be, you know, Rozyszcze. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the biggest, nearest city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck. It's between Lutsk and Kovel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lutsk? [LAUGHS] I wish I--

speaker: interviewer
text: L-U-T-S-K?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-K, yeah, Lutsk. Lutsk. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Poland up till 1939. And then the war start, 1939, the Russians took it over. And then the Germans came in in 1941. I think June 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And it was Poland, but it was part of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukraine, you know? But it was Polish police. Everything was Polish, you know, but it was more Ukrainian, you know, than-- It was all around-- it was a city around farms, and the city was, like, in the middle, you know? And were Jewish-- it was 80% Jewish people in the city, maybe more, you know? It was mixed, but it was more Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your childhood like in Rozyszcze?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning it was all right. But then-- then a German, you know, he burned the house. This was for Poland. I think it must have been in 19-- in 1933, you know? And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lucky, you know, I got out alive, and my mother and father. You know? And then after-- you know, my father was sleeping, you know, in the shop, you know, where there was left a little. And I guess he got sick, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother and I slept at a neighbor, you know, for a few days. And after we-- we moved-- we-- to a place. And it was like a cellar, where we lived. You know, like in the basement. And after, it took a year or two, and we rebuilt the house. But it wasn't the same place. It was down the hill a little, you know? Now, tell me a little bit, before we get to that, about when you were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were growing up in Rozyszcze. What was it like? I mean, what was Jewish life like? Was your family observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. They were Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your parents' first names. Your father's name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Herschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Herschel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Herschel. And my mother's name was Razel, you know. And I-- and I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were-- they were nice people. You know, my father worked-- he was a blacksmith, and my mother was a housewife. And I had a good life up till the house burned down, till everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before that, though, what was-- what made life good? Did you go to shul? Did you have Friday night dinners? Tell me a little bit about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. You mean before the house burned down?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What was your life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before the house burned down, I was too young, I think. You know? I remember-- you know-- I remember the cousins used to come. They used to stay over, you know, from Kovel, and one from [INAUDIBLE], you know, was another-- another city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember one cousin, her name was [INAUDIBLE]. She made cookies. And I went, I grabbed a cookie, and she didn't want to give it to me. She said, it's for shabbos. So when I told my father, so he said, go tell her to eat all the cookies or take the cookies and go home. You know? So I remember, you know? Do you remember shabbos dinners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, shabbos dinner, you know. Used to make, you know, like chicken soup and everything. And my father used to go to shul. We used to go to shul. He used to invite, you know, like a poor person for dinner, for-- you know, for Friday night, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And sometimes they used to stay over, sleep over. And sometime they used to go back. They used-- in the city used to be a-- a place where the poor people used to come in. They used to stay free overnight, over shabbos, you know? So-- Do you remember any other Jewish holidays as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, you know? It was [INAUDIBLE]-- The-- the synagogues used to be packed, you know, with-- with people, with the Jewish people and everything. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of your synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used-- they used-- there was a lot of synagogues in the city. It wasn't far from our house. They used to call it [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Synagogue. It was a little synagogue, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go there with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I always went with my father to the synagogue. You know? And I used to like, you know, [NON-ENGLISH], you know. They used to drink. They used to make vodka with honey. They used to dance on the table, you know. Sukkahs, you know? My father used to make a sukkah, you know, near the-- near the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And used to be a shamas, and he used to come and-- and [INAUDIBLE] with my mother. You know, where they shake it? You know? And it's like a-- like a lemon. You know, you hold it on the end. Yeah. And I remember, you know? And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after, you know, my father died. You know? He got sick. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- I think it was 1937 or 1938, you know, I believe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- so you were about 10 years old, maybe. Do you remember when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I was-- I was 13 years old. It was a night-- right after my bar mitzvah he died, you know? It could be 1938, because next year, you know, the Russian came in, in 1939. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about when your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember it was real, real-- it was-- it was real bad fast, because we didn't have too much, you know? And my mother, she used-- she used to take stuff, you know, and take it to some farm, and she would trade it for food, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And she used to bring it back, and she used to sell it. You know, like eggs. Or she used to get a chicken for the material, you know, for clothes, she used to take to the farmers. Or like a blouse or pants or shoes, or what. She used to trade. Or sometimes they used to buy it for money. You know? She would trade those clothings for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for-- for food. Yeah, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mom work after your dad passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's what she had to do. You know? That's the way. And it was-- it was bad, you know? We didn't have too much. But we managed, you know? And I still used to go to shul, you know. I used to go to shul every morning, you know, daven, you know, pray and everything. You know? And after, you know, I didn't-- I didn't go to school. You know, I think I went to school, you know, up till I was 10, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go to school? what was school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a cheder. It was-- you know, they-- they teach how to daven, our [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, I just start on one [NON-ENGLISH], two [NON-ENGLISH]. And then I stopped, you know? But they didn't teach how to read or-- I mean, read and write in Polish, you know, then it was. They just teach how to pray, you know, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you only went to cheder school? You didn't go to any other type of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- not to, like, elementary school or anything. You know? A private-- private school. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have no bar mitzvah. I didn't have no bar mitzvah because my father was sick. And-- and he taught me how to put on the [? tefillin ?] and everything. You know? Because, you know, like a rabbi, you had to pay, you know, everything. For them we couldn't. He was sick almost for three, four years. You know? And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think after he started spraying with blood. You know? He used to smoke a lot. And-- and when he slept outside, or from the smoke, everything, you know, he was-- he was real sick and everything. And I remember, you know, I went-- every Monday used to be a-- a bazaar. You know, the farmers, they used to bring in cows and sell, chicken, egg everything. You know, like-- so I went on the bazaar, and a guy came over to me and he said, you know, if I want to take the cow, you know, to the railroad, he'll give me 50 cents. You know, this how I used to make and buy a bread, buy something else. So I was afraid. He said, don't be afraid, you know. It's a real quiet cow. You just take it there. And I took it to the railroad, and somebody was waiting. He gave me 50 cents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the way home, I think I bought a bread. And then I bought some grapes. And it was, I think, a day before Yom Kippur. You know? And I brought it home to him. [SOBBING] And I give him the grape, and the next day he died. So I remember. You know? And the next day, we buried him in the night. It was Yom Kippur. You know? So I remember. And then my mother was crying and everything. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got up in the morning, you now, and I said, good morning. And he didn't answer. I went up, I lift up his head. He was still warm. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother went out. She started to yell, you know, and the neighbors came over and everything. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and after, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, I-- I had-- I worked in a-- in a mill a little, you know, where they make oil in the mill. They make flour too. And you had to turn this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, it's like poppy seed oil, sunflower oil, other oil. You know? And they used to pay me sometime 50 cents, sometime 25 cents, sometime a dollar. I mean, it wasn't American money. It was a zloty, Polish money, you know? But a bread was $0.15, $0.20. And a dozen eggs, you know, was maybe, $0.25, you know, whatever. And that's how we lived and that's how we survived. You know? And-- and after-- you know, I used to, when the Russians-- the next year, the Russians came in. You know? What do you remember about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Russians?

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before we get to that, though--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler's rise to power, I remember. You know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is when the house burned and everything, and the German, you know, he walked around. It-- it-- it wasn't the Germans yet, but a German lived in Poland, you know, right-- right next to our house. You know, and he always complained. He said the property, the house belonged to him. You know, before the German moved in, a Polish family lived-- lived there. And she sold the house to my father, and it was a little land too. So-- but my father didn't have the papers, you know, to show it belongs to him. And he couldn't get him out. That's because they burned the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who burnt your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know-- they said, you know, the German burned it. You know? What he-- what he claimed, it belongs to him. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see someone burn your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't see it. This was at night. You know, it was maybe about 1:00 or 2:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this, though? Was this in '39 or '40? Or much later?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was before. This was in-- in 1933 or '34. You know? This happened, you know, for Poland. It happened. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- so this is before you moved into it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before. It was in 1933. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But was your family living in this house at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, all the time, we lived in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were not in the house when it burned down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were in the house when it burned. You know? And our rabbi, you know-- it was after he was my rabbi in school, and-- and he went with another one. You know, they used to go a night to learn. So I guess he went home, and he saw the fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he run in and he woke us up. He carried me out from the house. You know? And [INAUDIBLE] my mother and father went out. You know? And everything burned. The only thing was left was the shop, you know? The blacksmith shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blacksmith shop was left. And this, I think, how he got sick. You know, to sleep there in-- and it was raining. It was cold. You know, it was around November. And it was-- that is what I think it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you rebuild the house? Or did you move to another house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they stayed in there, you know, so nobody would steal the whatever was left. But I-- I went to a neighbor. You know, I stayed there, I don't know, a few days, a week, until we got a place, what we moved in, in a-- in a basement. And we stayed there till another house was built. You know, we built another house. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between your family and non-Jewish families in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't too many Jewish families. You know, it was a Jewish city. It was-- most was Jewish people. And the relations with the non-Jewish families, they used to come into the shop, you know, non-Jewish families. They were all right. They were not rude.

speaker: interviewer
text: But a lot of times they used to call you, you damn Jew, whatever. You know, in Polish, [POLISH]. And the Ukrainians, you know, they used to call [POLISH]. They didn't like the Jewish people. And we used to go sometime, to the village, wherever they used to throw stones, you know, if they know you were Jewish. Whatever. You know? Did they throw stones at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, yeah. Yeah, I used to throw stones back on them. You know? And it used to be fights, a lot of times, between some Jewish and some Polish or Ukrainians. You know? But it was more Jewish people. And it was some Polish and Ukrainian living, but not around-- but not too many. It was a Jewish city. I think it was about 7,500 Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did life begin to change for you in 1939, when the Nazis invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first the Russians came in, in 1939. You know? And-- and they weren't too bad, the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was bad, because it was no bread. There was-- you had to stay in line for bread and everything. And then when you got to the-- sometime there was no bread. It was-- was real, real bad for us, you know? Well, then I was old already. I used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] work a little, go with a horse and wagon. I wish to work for people. And used to go to Lutsk. You know, we used to load flour and bring it to other cities and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and my mother, still she used to trade, you know? And after the-- the Russians, they got-- I had a cousin. He was a blacksmith, and he came to work in our shop. When I worked with him, I remember I couldn't lift a hammer yet, you know, to-- And-- but the Russians owned it. I mean, they didn't own it, but we worked for them. We didn't work for us. You know? So every-- every two weeks, you know, they used-- they used to bring some, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to get paid so much. And my cousin used to get-- used to get paid. In this way, you'd survive. But the Russians, they were all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they restrict your access or your movement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. I mean, you couldn't go from one city to another without-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you had to have a-- like a passport, you know, like, a document. You know? What are you doing in this city, whatever. You know, you couldn't go from one-- Even-- even when you used to work, a lot of time, they used to stop you. Where are you going? What are you doing? You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get into trouble with them, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never got into trouble with them. I never got into trouble with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you see any problems between the Russians and the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between the Russian and the Jews? No. Well, they-- they'll-- that's all?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you can keep talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? They left-- they-- when the Russians, when they-- when they came in, you know, they-- they took away from the rich ones. They-- they took away everything. They couldn't have no stores. They couldn't have anything. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish people. From the rich ones, they took away the stores. And I-- I guess they took it away from the rich to give it to the poor, you know? They took away some land from some, and they divided, you know, but-- but them. It was a-- a Jewish person. He had a lot a land, and I think they took it away from them. And a neighbor, he lived next door, and they had a big family. And they gave him so much land, where he could grow, you know, like potatoes or some other things, you know, for--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were nice to the Jewish people, except the rich ones, you know? But you couldn't go to synagogue. You couldn't pray. You weren't allowed to pray or do anything, you know, from-- Did you still keep the shabbos at your family, with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: We kept the shabbos, you know, but you had to work. You know, you weren't allowed to-- to keep holidays or anything. And Sunday was a free day, you know, for them. But if you wanted to work, you could work. You know? One day a week, so I guess they picked it Sunday, you know, whatever. But our city, it wasn't far, I think, from the border with the Germans. So there was a lot of Russians, a lot of soldiers, you know. It was going on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- but they didn't bother the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Did you see any refugees coming over the border from the German side of Poland into your area, which was controlled by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Some Jewish refugees did come over. Not too many. But a lot of times, you know, I think, they used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take-- they used to send them to Siberia a lot of times, Jewish refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember one refugee. He came over, and he-- and he said, you know, Jewish people, run. Run away. You know? Go to Russia. Go anyplace. Don't stay. The Germans, they'll be up here soon. You know? And he said, you know, in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Chelm-- a city-- this was on Pol-- on the German side already, in Poland-- they took some Jewish people. They told them to come, you know, to a synagogue. And the synagogue was [INAUDIBLE], you know? And after they brought, you know, some water. What do you call it? You know, a can of water? And they put so much water, you know, and the Jewish people, you know, they drown in the-- in the water, you know? I was listen when he was telling other people. You know, I was just listening. [INAUDIBLE] You didn't see this. He was just telling you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was telling us. You know? I didn't see it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape one, the interview with Morris Rubenstein.
